title: STANDARD FOR PITAHAYAS CXS 237-2003 Adopted in 2003. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR PITAHAYAS CXS 237-2003 Adopted in 2003. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document title and identification: 'Standard for Pitahayas CXS 237-2003', adopted 2003 and amended 2005 and 2011.

Scope and definition: applies to commercial species/varieties of pitahayas (genera Selenicereus and Hylocereus) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging, excluding pitahayas intended for industrial processing.
Introduces quality provisions for pitahayas, comprising minimum requirements and a three‑class classification system (Extra, Class I, Class II) with associated tolerances.

Lists minimum quality requirements for all classes: whole, sound (no rotting), clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, no abnormal external moisture, no foreign smell/taste, firm and fresh in appearance, no skin cracks, peduncle 15–25 mm, without thorns, and sufficiently developed/ripened to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

Defines classification into three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with progressively relaxed allowances for superficial defects while requiring that defects not affect the pulp, and provides notes on maturity assessment.

Extra Class: pitahayas must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I: pitahayas of good quality characteristic of the variety with slight allowed defects (e.g., slight shape defects or skin defects up to 1 cm2 of total surface) provided they do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping quality, presentation or the pulp.

Class II: includes pitahayas that meet minimum requirements but do not qualify for higher classes, allowing defects such as shape defects and skin defects up to 2 cm2 of the total surface area provided the pulp is not affected.
Size is determined by unit weight per Table 3, which assigns size codes A–I to specific gram ranges that differ for yellow versus red/white pitahayas.

Allows tolerances by package for produce not meeting the declared class: quality tolerances vary by class (Extra, I, II) as specified, and size tolerances allow 10% by number or weight of fruit of the immediately adjacent size(s); for yellow pitahayas the number of units per package may be used.

Specifies quality tolerances by class: Extra allows 5% by number/weight not meeting Extra but meeting Class I or its tolerances; Class I allows 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II or its tolerances; Class II allows 10% not meeting Class II or minimum requirements except for produce rendered unfit by rotting or similar deterioration.
Extra Class: up to 5% by number or weight may fail to meet Extra class but must meet Class I requirements or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
Class I: up to 10% by number or weight may fail to meet Class I but must meet Class II requirements or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

Class II: up to 10% by number or weight may fail to meet Class II or the minimum requirements, except produce affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption is excluded.

Size tolerances: for all classes, up to 10% by number or weight may be of the size immediately above and/or below the declared size, and for yellow pitahayas the number of units per package may be used.

Presentation requirements: packages must be uniform (same origin, variety/commercial type, quality, colour and size) with visible contents representative of the whole; pitahayas must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean materials that do not cause damage (non-toxic inks/glues allowed), in accordance with CXC 44-1995, and containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance criteria and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Each package must contain pitahayas uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality, colour and size, and the visible portion of the contents must be representative of the entire package.

Packaging must protect pitahayas using internal materials that are new, clean and of a quality that avoids external or internal damage, printing/labeling must use non-toxic ink or glue, and packing must comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers must meet required quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure suitable handling, shipping and preservation of pitahayas, and packages must be free of foreign matter and smell.

Labelling must follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) plus specific provisions: consumer packages and non‑retail containers must indicate produce name (if not visible) and may include variety; non‑retail containers must show exporter/packer/dispatcher identification, produce commercial type (colour), origin, commercial identification (class, size code or weight range, optional number of units and net weight), and may bear an official inspection mark.
Consumer packages must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and the additional specific provisions referenced in this standard.
If the produce is not visible from the outside, the consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may state the variety and/or commercial type.
Non‑retail containers must bear specified particulars legibly and indelibly grouped on the same side and visible from the outside, or those particulars must appear in accompanying shipment documents.
Identification requirement: each package must show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher and may include an optional identification code.
Nature of produce: packages must indicate the name of the produce if contents are not visible and the commercial type defined by skin colour (yellow, red or white).
Origin of produce: packages must state the country of origin and may optionally include the district or national/regional/local place name where grown.
Commercial identification: packages must declare the class and size (size code or weight range in grams) and may optionally show number of units and net weight.
Only a heading indicating an optional official inspection mark is provided; no substantive provisions are given in this section.

Introduces contaminant requirements for the produce, directing compliance with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins (CXS 193-1995) and Codex pesticide residue limits, and includes editorial footnotes (e.g., on recycled food-grade material and other notes).

sections.section: 7.1 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).
sections.summary: Requires that the produce comply with the maximum contaminant and toxin levels set out in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and notes that this includes recycled material of food-grade quality.

sections.section: 7.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Mandates that the produce comply with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends handling and preparation in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and related Codex texts, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.
